Code Explorer

main.py + test_main.py

Project: lab8-pygame | Focus: moving squares Part B | Files analyzed: main.py, test_main.py, README.md

Project Snapshot

This explorer maps the full runtime loop of the Lab 8 moving squares project. The animation is driven by main(), which polls input, updates square motion, and redraws the screen. The most important state lives in the Square and GameState dataclasses.

Chosen data flows: square.vx / square.vy (score 4), dt_seconds (score 4), and state.global_speed (score 3). These variables are central because they are created, transformed, and then used to change visible motion on screen.

Call Graph
This is the bird's-eye view: input, update, and drawing stay separate, which makes the program easier to read and test.
Dependency Graph
The project depends mostly on standard library modules plus Pygame, while tests depend directly on the game logic in main.py.
Full Frame Sequence
Every frame follows the same pattern: read events, update state with time, draw the latest state, and flip the frame to the screen.
Square Update Sequence
A single square first gets random jitter, then flee steering, then movement, rotation, and finally wall-bounce correction.
Flee Behavior Sequence
Smaller squares scan nearby bigger squares, build one escape direction, blend toward it, and clamp the final speed.
Velocity Data Flow
The velocity vector is created once, then nudged by jitter and flee logic before it drives visible movement each frame.
Delta Time Data Flow
dt_seconds starts at the clock and then affects randomness, movement, and rotation so animation stays time-based.
Global Speed Data Flow
Keyboard input can change the shared global speed, which then scales both translation and rotation for every square.
Good Patterns
✅ Small focused functions
Functions such as handle_input(), update_game_state(), and draw_scene() each do one clear job.
BasicsYou can learn the game loop one step at a time.
EngineeringFocused functions are easier to test and easier to change safely.
ArchitectureThis keeps input, simulation, and rendering loosely separated.
Read about keeping methods small
✅ Typed state models
The Square and GameState dataclasses make the moving pieces of the program explicit.
BasicsIt is easier to see what data each square needs.
EngineeringDataclasses reduce boilerplate and make test setup cleaner.
ArchitectureState is modeled as data, not hidden inside unrelated functions.
Read the Python dataclasses docs
✅ Named tuning constants
Movement ranges, flee distance, jitter size, and frame limits live in named constants at the top of the file.
BasicsYou can tune gameplay without hunting through every function.
EngineeringClear constants reduce magic numbers and improve readability.
ArchitectureGame rules are centralized, which supports later balancing work.
Read about symbolic constants
Potential Issues
⚠️ Render work repeats
draw_square() creates a fresh surface and rotates it every frame for every square.
BasicsThe picture is redrawn correctly, but extra work happens each tick.
EngineeringRepeated allocation and rotation will become a hotspot as square count grows.
ArchitectureA render cache would separate visual assets from simulation rules.
Cache base square surfaces by size and color if you decide to scale the project up.
⚠️ One file owns all layers
main.py currently holds constants, data models, input, simulation, rendering, and program startup.
BasicsThis is fine for a lab, but it gets harder to navigate as features grow.
EngineeringLarge single files make merge conflicts and targeted testing more likely.
ArchitectureSplitting into modules would make the project easier to extend later.
A natural next split is models.py, logic.py, and rendering.py.
5/5
Strong type hint coverage.
Parameters, returns, dataclass fields, and concrete containers are all used consistently in the main game logic.

Strengths

The project uses explicit field types in both dataclasses, which makes the moving state easy to inspect and explain.

Function signatures also use concrete generics such as list[Square] and tuple[int, int, int], so the shape of data stays visible from call to call.

Gaps To Improve

A small type alias like Color = tuple[int, int, int] would make repeated RGB tuples easier to read.

The random provider is effectively duck-typed in tests, so a tiny Protocol could document the random() and uniform() methods that jitter needs.

Overlap fallback biases flee direction
medium
Perfect overlap always pushes the square to the right for one step.
Per-frame surface allocation
medium
Drawing creates and rotates a new surface for every square every frame.
Quadratic flee scan
medium
Each square scans all other squares during flee behavior.
Mixed time units
low
Movement values act like frame units, then are scaled back with FPS.
Single-file growth risk
low
The lab is understandable now, but new features will crowd main.py.
Wins
Time-based frame control
clock.tick(FPS) caps the frame rate and also produces dt_seconds, so the animation is more stable across machines.
Speed limits stay bounded
clamp_velocity_to_max_speed() protects each square from runaway values after jitter or flee blending.
Risks
Fresh surfaces per square
draw_square() allocates a new alpha surface and rotates it every frame, which is the clearest future hotspot.
O n squared flee logic
apply_flee_behavior() compares each square with every other square, which is fine for 20 objects but will not scale smoothly.
4 / 6 Performance tab included because the project has clear runtime tradeoffs. Top finding: rendering creates new surfaces each frame.
1
Study dataclasses
This will help you explain why Square and GameState are easier to work with than loose dictionaries.
2
Read Pygame transform docs
Useful for understanding the cost of rotation and how pygame.transform.rotate() affects rendering.
3
Review math.hypot
This function appears several times and is the clean way to compute vector length for speed and distance.
4
Learn single responsibility
A good next design lesson if you later split main.py into smaller modules.
5
Explore steering behavior
The flee logic is a simple steering rule, so this gives you the bigger idea behind the Part B behavior.
